headline: New England Forests Rise Again: 60% Coverage After Near-Complete Loss
meta_description: Once nearly cleared by the mid-1800s, New England has achieved remarkable forest restoration in one of America's most densely populated regions.
summary_short: New England's forests have made a stunning comeback, reclaiming 60% coverage despite being almost entirely cleared two centuries ago.
body:
In the mid-1800s, New England stood nearly bare. Centuries of logging and agricultural expansion had stripped the landscape of its natural forest cover, transforming one of America's most densely populated regions into open land. Yet today, the region tells a different story: forests now blanket approximately 60% of New England's territory.

This recovery represents one of the most dramatic ecological comebacks in the modern era. As agricultural practices shifted and land use evolved, trees gradually reclaimed the landscape. Forests moved back in where farms had fallen away, green spreading across hills and valleys that had been brown for generations.

The transformation demonstrates nature's resilience when given space to recover. In a densely populated part of the country, with millions of people living nearby, this return of forest shows that even in heavily used regions, large-scale ecological restoration is possible. New England's resurgent woodlands now support wildlife, improve air quality, and store carbon—a living reminder that landscapes can heal.
